ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for beef noodles with pickled cabbage; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup dish — composite mixed dish with meat and vegetables in broth
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle soup dish) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 ancestor for a broth-based noodle dish with beef and pickled vegetables. No exact FoodEx2 entry for this specific dish exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for beef noodles with pickled cabbage as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beef noodles with pickled cabbage weighs approximately 600 g and is the typical single-serve portion as sold at hawker stalls and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food (Beef Noodles / Noodle Soup with Beef and Vegetables)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB entry for 'beef noodles with pickled cabbage' exists. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for beef noodle soup and supplemented with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) component data for pickled cabbage (sauerkraut/mustard greens). The input record's per-100g values (energy 108.33 kcal, protein 6.67 g, fat 4.17 g, carbohydrate 10.83 g) are consistent with HPB-range estimates for broth-based beef noodle dishes and have been retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamins A/B-group, vitamin D/E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed values are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — wheat noodles / Asian noodle soup analogous entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef noodles with pickled cabbage. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat-based Asian noodles in broth (GI range 40–60 for boiled wheat noodles). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 64.98 g carbohydrate in 600 g serving) / 100 ≈ 34.4, rounded to 34.3. The presence of pickled cabbage (acetic acid) and protein from beef may modestly lower the effective GI; the estimate is conservative.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pickled cabbage, soy sauce, and savoury broth (366.67 mg sodium per 100 g; ~2200 mg per 600 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request broth on the side, reduce pickled cabbage quantity, and ask for reduced soy sauce to keep the per-meal sodium intake below the recommended 600 mg limit; the full bowl as served provides approximately 370% of that target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles with low dietary fibre (0.83 g/100 g) produce a low GI (~53) and a high glycaemic load (~34 per bowl), driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to half, increase the beef and vegetable ratio, and consider requesting konjac or lower-GI noodle substitutes; the acetic acid in pickled cabbage may modestly blunt postprandial glucose response and should be retained.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef (1.67 g/100 g; ~10 g per bowl), trans fat (0.08 g/100 g), and high sodium (~2200 mg per bowl) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to half a bowl, choose lean beef cuts, omit chilli oil, and request reduced-sodium broth to minimise combined saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; high sodium from pickled cabbage may worsen tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with fish or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid load, limit pickled cabbage quantity, and omit chilli oil if nightshade sensitivity is present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef noodles with pickled cabbage (酸菜牛肉面, Suān cài niúròu miàn) is a popular Chinese-origin noodle dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and through food delivery platforms. The dish consists of springy boiled wheat noodles served in a savoury, soy-seasoned beef broth, topped with tender sliced beef and tangy pickled cabbage (suan cai or mustard greens). The broth is typically flavoured with ginger, garlic, and soy sauce, giving it a deep umami character. The pickled cabbage contributes a distinctive sour crunch that contrasts with the richness of the beef and broth. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 108 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 4.2 g fat (1.7 g saturated), and 10.8 g carbohydrate with only 0.8 g dietary fibre. A standard 600 g bowl delivers roughly 650 kcal and approximately 2200 mg sodium — well above the recommended single-meal sodium limit for hypertensive individuals. The glycaemic index is estimated at 53 (low) based on wheat noodle analogues, with a high glycaemic load of ~34 per bowl due to the large noodle portion. Clinically, the dish is of concern for hypertension (very high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high GL, low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium). The acetic acid in pickled cabbage may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response. The dish is not suitable for strict halal diets unless certified beef is used, and contains gluten and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for beef noodles with pickled cabbage as a named composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB beef noodle soup data and USDA SR Legacy component data for pickled vegetables. The input record's per-100g values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite broth-based noodle dish are not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 bowl / 600 g' entries); only one is retained. 3. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was cited under the HPB Singapore organisation; this has been preserved as the nutrient source reference. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.08 g/100 g from the input record; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace trans fats in beef and is retained. 5. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains beef whose halal certification cannot be confirmed from the available data; consumers should verify with the vendor. 6. GI glycaemic load per serving calculated as (53 × 64.98) / 100 = 34.4 ≈ 34.3 (available carbohydrate = 10.83 g/100 g × 600 g serving = 64.98 g). 7. ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed match_type enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values. Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 53 falls into the low category (GI <= 55).

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' — 'analogous_estimate' is not a valid match_type value; allowed values are ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. Updated gap note to clarify that closest_parent is used because no exact FoodEx2 entry exists for this specific dish.
Iteration 3: Confirmed foodex2_match_type is now 'closest_parent'. All hard gates verified. No further changes required.
